title: McIlroy Makes History: Back-to-Back Masters Glory
excerpt: Rory McIlroy claims his second Green Jacket in as many years, edging Scottie Scheffler by one shot at Augusta National to become the fourth back-to-back Masters champion.
body:
McIlroy Captures Second Straight Green Jacket

Rory McIlroy etched his name into Masters history on Sunday, winning the 90th edition of the tournament in succession to claim his sixth major championship title. The defending champion held steady against an elite field at Augusta National, posting a closing 71 for 12-under par and a one-shot victory over Scottie Scheffler, whose bogey-free 68 left him at 11-under. It was a dramatic, improbable Sunday marked by miraculous par saves, brilliant escapes from trouble, and one final triumph that sent McIlroy to tears as he hugged his daughter Poppy on the final green—just as he had a year earlier.

Winning Score
12-under
Victory Margin
1 shot
McIlroy Major Count
6th
Back-to-Back Winners
4 all-time

Amen Corner Masterclass

The pivotal moment came at the infamous 12th hole, the "Golden Bell." McIlroy waited for perfect wind conditions, then delivered a 9-iron inside 7 feet—the closest approach of the day at that treacherous par-three. He converted the putt to reach 13-under, two clear of his nearest competitors. From there, a pair of birdie conversions at the 12th and 13th pushed him further clear. Scheffler mounted a spirited challenge with brilliant shots at the 15th and 16th, but McIlroy's unshakeable nerve and intimate knowledge of Augusta's slopes, particularly on the greens, carried him home.

A Historic Fraternity

With this victory, McIlroy joined an exclusive group: Jack Nicklaus, Nick Faldo and Tiger Woods are the only other players to have won back-to-back Masters titles. It is only the second time since Tiger Woods (2001-2002) that a player has captured consecutive Green Jackets—a testament to the ferocity of the modern game at Augusta. This is also McIlroy's 30th victory on the PGA Tour and his sixth major championship, moving him into a tie for 12th all-time in major victories.

The Chasing Pack

Justin Rose, the 45-year-old English veteran, produced a dramatic back-nine assault with four birdies in five holes, finishing tied third at 10-under alongside Cameron Young, Sam Burns, Tyrrell Hatton and Russell Henley. Rose's performance was nothing short of inspiring—his ninth consecutive top-25 finish at Augusta, defying conventional wisdom about late-career form. Young's course-management and iron play made him a factor throughout; Burns' 60-foot bomb at the 16th set the crowd alight even as the outcome grew clearer.

What's Next

McIlroy pockets $4.5m for his efforts and a place in golf history. Scheffler, despite his dominance all week, leaves empty-handed but with a resume that continues to impress with each passing fortnight. Next up: the PGA Tour pivots to the RBC Heritage at Hilton Head and beyond as the season builds towards the U.S. Open. McIlroy's closing remarks—"I don't want to stop here"—signal intent of more majors to follow in this remarkable era.
